coredata.dc:title: In situ synthesized carbon quantum dots@metal organic framework via corroding Ni foam for efficient oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuzhen
Zhou, Huajun
Zhao, Peihua
Yuan, Kai
Zhou, Rui
Gao, Hui
Qu, Yongping
Wang, Yanzhong
coredata.dc:description: The rational design of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) electrocatalysts is crucial for efficient oxygen evolution reaction (OER). However, the poor conductivity and stability of MOFs impede their applications in water oxidation. In this paper, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)@Ni-Fe thiophene-2,5-dicarboxylic acid (NiFe-TDC) composite catalyst is synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method by using ferric citrate and thiophene-2,5-dicarboxylic acid (H2TDC) to corrode Ni foam (NF). Due to the direct etching of NF, the prepared CQDs@NiFe-TDC exhibits the robust structure and tightly combine with NF, in which CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits excellent oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity in alkaline solution, with an overpotential of only 222 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and stable operation for 100 h at 100 mA cm−2. Furthermore, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 maintains the outstanding OER performance in seawater with 1 mol/L KOH, with an overpotential of 237 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The assembled CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03//Pt/C water electrolysis cells achieve 100 mA cm−2 at a low voltage of 1.93 V. The experimental results show that the excellent OER performance of CQD@NiFe-TDC is attributed to the improved electrical conductivity and modulated electronic structure of MOFs by the incorporation of CQDs. This work provides a simple and cost-effective method for the preparation of CQD@MOF composites to achieve efficient and stable water oxidation materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen evolution reaction
Carbon quantum dots
Electrocatalysis
Metal organic frameworks

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the following research projects: Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (20210302123049), Key R&D Projects in Shanxi Province (202102090301024, 202102090301025, 202202030201003, 202202090301022), Special Project of Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange of Shanxi Province (202204041101003), Shanxi Technology Innovation Center for Co-processing and Resource Utilization of Industrial Solid Waste (202104010911016), Youth Project of Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2022QE108), Key R & D Projects of Shanxi Province (202302030201002), and National Defense Basic Research Program (JCKY2023408C012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis diagram of procedure for CQD@NiFe-TDC-X.

(a-c) SEM images of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 at different magnifications, (d) TEM images of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, (e) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images, (f) corresponding to the lattice fringe pattern after inverse Fourier transform, and (g) EDX mapping.

(a) XRD patterns of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, NiFe-TDC and CQD, (b) Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and (c) Raman spectra of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 and NiFe-TDC, (d) the XPS survey spectra, high resolution XPS of (e) Ni 2p and (f) Fe 2p of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 and NiFe-TDC.

OER performance analysis of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, NiFe-TDC, CQD (a) LSV curves, (b) the overpotential comparison, (c) the Tafel slope curves, (d) Nyquist plots, (e) C dl plots, (f) TOF values corresponding at 1.50 V, (g) the durability test of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 at a current density 100 mA cm−2, (h) comparison of overpotentials of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 with the recently published literature under 10 mA cm−2.

(a-b) SEM images and (c) XRD patterns of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 after the OER stability test, (d-f) corresponding high resolution spectra of S 2p, Ni 2p and Fe 2p of the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 before and after OER stability test.

(a) OER polarization curves of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 in 1.0 M KOH containing different amounts of NaCl, (b) OER polarization curves of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 in 1.0 M KOH and 1.0 M KOH + seawater, (c) the overpotentials comparison of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 at 10, 50 and 100 mA cm−2 under different test conditions and alkaline seawater solution, (d) The stability test of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 in alkaline seawater electrolytes, (e) the comparison of LSV curves of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 after the stability test in alkaline seawater solution.

(a) LSV curves of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03//Pt/C and RuO2//Pt/C overall water splitting test, (b) Chronopotentiometry curve of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03//Pt/C at 100 mA cm−2, (c) LSV curves of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03//Pt/C post 100 h of durability testing.

head.ce:title: In situ synthesized carbon quantum dots@metal organic framework via corroding Ni foam for efficient oxygen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Efficient Hydrogen Storage & Production Technology and Application, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Efficient Hydrogen Storage & Production Technology and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Efficient Hydrogen Storage & Production Technology and Application, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China (Y.Z. Wang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational design of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) electrocatalysts is crucial for efficient oxygen evolution reaction (OER). However, the poor conductivity and stability of MOFs impede their applications in water oxidation. In this paper, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)@Ni-Fe thiophene-2,5-dicarboxylic acid (NiFe-TDC) composite catalyst is synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method by using ferric citrate and thiophene-2,5-dicarboxylic acid (H2TDC) to corrode Ni foam (NF). Due to the direct etching of NF, the prepared CQDs@NiFe-TDC exhibits the robust structure and tightly combine with NF, in which CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits excellent oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity in alkaline solution, with an overpotential of only 222 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and stable operation for 100 h at 100 mA cm−2. Furthermore, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 maintains the outstanding OER performance in seawater with 1 mol/L KOH, with an overpotential of 237 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The assembled CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03//Pt/C water electrolysis cells achieve 100 mA cm−2 at a low voltage of 1.93 V. The experimental results show that the excellent OER performance of CQD@NiFe-TDC is attributed to the improved electrical conductivity and modulated electronic structure of MOFs by the incorporation of CQDs. This work provides a simple and cost-effective method for the preparation of CQD@MOF composites to achieve efficient and stable water oxidation materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of fossil fuels causes significant pollution and environmental issues, making the search for efficient and clean alternative energy sources a major societal priority. [1,2] Hydrogen energy is gaining attention due to its environmental friendliness and high energy density. [3,4] Utilizing catalysts for electrolytic hydrogen production in wastewater or seawater systems is considered one of the methods for producing high-purity hydrogen. However, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), one of the half-reactions in water electrolysis, involves a four-electron process with a slow reaction rate, which significantly limits the efficiency of water electrolysis. [5,6] Therefore, developing catalysts with lower OER overpotentials is of great importance. Currently, excellent catalysts such as IrO2 and RuO2 based on precious metals show the promising OER performance, but their high production costs make them unsuitable for widespread application. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find inexpensive and efficient OER electrocatalysts. [7–10].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of crystalline porous materials with periodic network structures formed by the ordered self-assembly of organic ligands and metal ions. Owing to their extensive surface area, rich reactive sites, and structural diversity, MOFs have been widely applied in the field of electrocatalysis. [11,12] However, most MOFs cannot be directly used as catalysts in practical applications mainly due to their low electronic conductivity and poor stability, hindering their application in electrocatalysis. [13–15] Therefore, searching suitable methods to enhance the electrocatalytic performance of MOFs-based materials is of great significance for the development of MOFs-based electrocatalytic materials.

Presently, many strategies have been used to improve the electronic conductivity of MOFs. One strategy involves the design and synthesis of novel MOFs with high electrical conductivity. [16–18] For instance, Fu et al. [19] successfully synthesized ultrathin 2D conductive cobalt-hexaaminobenzene coordination polymer (Co-HAB) nanosheets at room temperature. Due to the ultrathin nanostructure and the utilization of π-d conjugated conductive network, the dense stacking of π orbitals between adjacent layers is achieved, leading to the generation of abundant charge carriers and consequently exhibiting high electrical conductivity, thereby aiding in improving the electrocatalytic performance of OER. Another strategy involves the incorporation of conductive materials into MOFs, such as carbon-based materials (graphene, carbon nanotubes and activated carbon), [20,21] conductive polymers (polypyrrole and polyaniline), [22,23] and other conductive materials. [24–26] For instance, Liu et al. [14] prepared a composite material of MOFs and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which showed an overpotential of as low as 240 mV at 10 mA cm−2. It is worth noting that the activity of oxalate-based Ni-Fe MOF/CNT hybrid composites is much higher than that of Ni-Fe MOFs alone. On the one hand, it is due to the synergistic effect of MOFs and CNTs, which exposes more active sites. On the other hand, the presence of carbon nanotubes compensates for the weak conductivity of MOFs and accelerates the electron transfer rate, thereby improving the catalytic performance of OER. Similarly, Mahrokh et al. [27] employed a solvothermal method to grow FeNiCo-MIL on MXene. The introduction of MXene not only prevents the aggregation of MOFs particles but also enhances the electrical conductivity of the catalyst, further facilitating the entry of water electrolyte into numerous active sites of the catalyst and accelerating the charge transfer rate, thereby exhibiting the excellent OER electrocatalytic activity in alkaline media. Therefore, introducing conductive nanomaterials into MOFs is a practical and effective method to enhance the OER performance of MOFs.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a class of 0D carbon-based nanomaterials (particles size < 10 nm) have attracted intensive attention in the energy conversion and storage due to their good chemical stability, high electronic conductivity, and rich functional groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl). [28,29] The negatively charged oxygen groups of CQDs can result in the formation chemical bonds with organic ligands and metal ions, which would regulate the morphology of MOFs and improve the hydrophilicity of composites. Encapsulating CQD into the pores of MOFs can significantly improve the electron transfer efficiency and electrocatalytic activity of the material. [30,31] Therefore, the combination of CQDs and MOFs provides a feasible approach to enhance the OER catalytic activity. For example, Hong et al. [32] inserted carbon dots (CDs) into cobalt-based zeolitic imidazolate framework (Co-ZIF) nanosheet arrays (Co-ZIF/CDs/CC). CDs induce the generation of Co-ZIF with higher surface area, charge transfer capacity, and coordination unsaturated metal sites, affecting its mass diffusion capacity, promoting the transformation of Co-ZIF to amorphous CoOOH active phase during OER, and the coupling of CDs with CoOOH further regulates the OER catalytic performance. Similarly, Rezaei et al. [33] synthesized composite materials of layered NiCo-MOF and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) via electrodeposition strategy. The introduction of graphene quantum dots enhances the conductivity of the catalyst itself and further optimizes the activation energy during the adsorption–desorption process. Therefore, CQDs can not only improve the electronic conductivity of MOFs materials but also optimize their morphology, and thus improving their OER performance. However, the synthesis of CQDs@MOFs composite catalytic materials is more complicated and usually requires multiple steps in previous reports, which is not conducive to large-scale production. Therefore, it is particularly important to develop a simple method to synthesize CQDs@MOFs composite catalytic materials with high OER performance.

Based on the above research, we proposed a new strategy to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs)@nickel–iron thiophene-based framework (NiFe-TDC) via one-pot solvothermal method by using ferric citrate and thiophene-2,5-dicarboxylic acid (H2TDC). Under high temperature conditions, H2TDC will ionize H+ and TDC2−, while ferric citrate will ionize Fe3+ and (C6H5O7)3−. The released H+ and Fe3+ ions will etch NF to produce Ni2+. The formed TDC2− ions will coordinate with Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions to form NiFe-TDC, while (C6H5O7)3− will be carbonized to form CQDs, thereby forming CQD@NiFe-TDC composites on NF. CQDs can not only improve the electronic conductivity of MOFs, but also enhance their hydrophilicity due to the abundant −O groups on the surface of CQDs. Simultaneously, the large number of-COOH groups on the surface of CQDs can accelerate the ability of proton transfer in the OER process, thereby improving the catalytic performance of OER. In addition, the effects of solvent and ferric citrate concentration on the morphology and electrochemical properties of CQD@NiFe-TDC have been investigated. The experimental results indicate that CQD@NiFe-TDC with ferric citrate concentration of 0.03 M has low overpotentials of 222 and 258 mV for OER at current densities of 10 and 100 mA cm−2, respectively, and it can run stably for 100h at 100 mA cm−2 current density. Moreover, the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 electrode has a low overpotential of 237 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 for seawater oxidation. Furthermore, a constructed water electrolysis device with CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 as the positive electrode and Pt/C as the negative electrode can be achieved with only 1.52 V of voltage at a current density of 10 mA cm−2.
All chemicals are of chemical analytical purity, and detailed specification parameters are provided in the support information.

In a routine experimental, NF (1 × 3 cm2) was undergoing ultrasonic cleaned using 3 M HCl followed by the treatment with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, respectively. Subsequently, it was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6 h. Ferric citrate and H2TDC (2 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water (DI) and anhydrous ethanol (EA) mixed solution (volume ratio of 1:1). The cleaned NF was placed in a mixed solution and transferred to a reactor with polytetrafluoroethylene lining, and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the NF was washed several times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The obtained product was recorded as CQD@NiFe-TDC-X (X represents the concentration of ferric citrate, which are 0.01, 0.03 and 0.05 M, respectively). For comparison, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 was synthesized using 30 mL of deionized water and 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol at the same experimental conditions, which represented D-CQD@NiFe-TDC and E-CQD@NiFe-TDC, respectively. In addition, CQDs were synthesized by only using ferric citrate, and NiFe-TDC were synthesized with ion nitrate and H2TDC at the same experimental conditions with CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03.
The morphology, structure and composition were analyzed by using SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR and Raman, and the detailed parameters were provided in the supporting information.
All electrochemical tests utilized typical three-electrode and two-electrode water splitting systems. Specific experimental details and parameters are available in the supporting information.

The synthesis process of CQD@NiFe-TDC nanoflower arrays via a one-pot hydrothermal method was illustrated in Fig. 1 . In a solvent thermal process under high temperature and pressure, ferric citrate is dissociated into Fe3+ and (C6H5O7)3−, and H2TDC is dissociated into H+ and TDC2−. H+ and Fe3+ can simultaneously corrode the NF and produce Ni2+. Then, Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions are coordinated with TDC2− to form NiFe thiophene-based MOFs, and citrate ions are carbonization and polymerized into CQDs. The specific reaction process is as follows:
FeC6H5O7 → (C6H5O7)3− + Fe3+(1)
H2TDC → H+ + TDC2−(2)
H+ +Fe3+ + Ni → H2 + Fe2++Ni2+(3)
Fen++ Ni2+ + TDC2− → NiFe-TDC(4)

The morphology of CQD@NiFe-TDC is analyzed by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a-c, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits the hydrangea-like structure with nanoflowers, which uniformly coats on the surface of NF substrate. In contrast, when iron nitrate is added instead of ferric citrate, NiFe-TDC exhibits a rod-like structure (Fig. S1a). However, with only adding ferric citrate, nanoparticles are formed and unevenly dispersed on the surface of NF, indicating the formation of CQDs (Fig. S1b). In addition, the effects of solvents and ferric citrate concentrations on the morphology are explored. When the deionized water is used as the solvent, the formed MOFs nanoneedles are sporadically distributed on the surface of NF (Fig. S1c). In contrast, when anhydrous ethanol is used as the solvent, no MOFs are generated on NF (Fig. S1d). Therefore, the samples prepared in a mixed solvent of deionized water and ethanol were further investigated. When the ferric citrate concentration is 0.01 M, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.01 exhibits a nanoneedle-like structure (Fig. S1e). With further increasing up to 0.05 M, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.05 indicates the thick sheet-like structure and serious aggregation (Fig. S1f), which is unfavorable to the exposure of electrochemical performance active sites. Therefore, the as-prepared CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 was further investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As demonstrated by Fig. 2d, it further reaffirms the ultra-thin nanosheets structure of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, and many nanoparticles with average diameter of 2–3 nm were uniformly dispersed on the surface of NiFe-TDC. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2e-f) display the distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.30 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of carbons. [34] The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping (Fig. 2g-k) demonstrates the uniform distribution of Fe, Ni, S, C and O elements on CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, indicating the successful synthesis of CQD and NiFe-TDC.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to examine the crystal structures of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, NiFe-TDC and CQD. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the main peaks at 9.04°, 14.52° and 18.42° correspond to the (020), (110) and (040) planes of NiFe-TDC, wihch mathes well with the simulated XRD pattern (CCDC no. 716347), indicating the successful synthesis of NiFe-TDC. Comparing with the pristine of NiFe-TDC, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits higher peak intensity, implying that the formation of CQD is conducive to enhance the crystallinity of NiFe-TDC. No obvious peaks corresponding to carbon were observed for the pristine CQD, indicating the low crystallinity of CQD. The surface functional groups and coordination energies of the materials were analyzed by using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3b, the eminent peaks at 1570 and 1370 cm−1 in CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 are attributed to the non-symmetric and symmetric stretching oscillations of carboxylate bands. [35,36] The peak at 3595 cm−1 is ascribed to the –OH group within water molecules. In comparison to NiFe-TDC, the disappearance of the characteristic peak at 808 cm−1 (C–H) in CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 indicates the formation of a robust coordination bond between CQD and NiFe-TDC. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 3c, the peak at 1468 cm−1 corresponds to the thiophene ring group [37], and the peak at 810 cm−1 is assigned to the C-O-Ni (Fe) bond [38], indicating the effectual synthesis of MOFs. The G and D peaks of carbon materials overlapped with the peaks of COO– at 1587 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 in MOFs. Compared with NiFe-TDC, these peak intensity of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 is higher, which is due to the insertion of CQDs into NiFe-TDC. The peaks at 499 and 687 cm−1 in CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 were attributed to the Ni-O and Fe-O bonds [39]. Compared to NiFe-TDC, the stronger vibration signals of Fe-O bonds in CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 indicate that CQDs facilitates the formation of Fe-O bonds and promotes the synthesis of MOFs. Furthermore, the chemical states of the surface elements and their interactions were analyzed by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 3d-f). The XPS spectra (Fig. 3d) confirmed the presence of Fe, Ni, S, O and C elements, which is consistent with the results of EDX. As shown in Fig. 3e, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p in CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 can be divided into six distinct peaks. The peaks at 853.34 and 870.69 eV correspond to Ni0 2p3/2 and Ni0 2p1/2 [40], and the peaks at 856.04 and 873.8 eV are attributed to Ni2+ 2p3/2 and Ni2+ 2p1/2 [41], indicating the existence of Ni mainly in 0 valence and + 2 valence in CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03. In addition, the peaks at 861.25 and 879.13 eV represent the satellite peaks of Ni 2p. For the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f), the peaks at 709.83 and 723.39 eV were assigned to Fe2+ 2p3/2 and Fe2+ 2p1/2 [42], while the other double peaks at 713.11 eV and 725.93 eV belong to Fe3+ 2p3/2 and Fe3+ 2p1/2 [43]。Notably, the peaks of Ni 2p and Fe 2p shift high binding energies after the introduction of CQDs, indicating that the interfacial interactions between CQDs and NiFe-TDC can modulate the electronic structure of nickel and iron ions, which results in the formation of high-valence nickel and iron ions and thus enhancing the OER catalytic performance. In the S 2p spectrum (Fig. S2a), the peaks at 163.98 and 165.23 eV correspond to C-S-C (2p3/2 and 2p1/2) bonding within the thiophene ring, and the peak at 162.21 eV was correspond to M−S (M: Ni, Fe). [40,44] The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2b) exhibited three peaks at 284.80, 286.48 and 288.52 eV, which are assigned to C–C, C-O and O-C=O. [41] In the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2c), the peaks at 530.64, 531.54 and 532.32 eV are attributed to M−O (M = Ni or Fe), O-C=O and O–H, respectively. [44].

The electrical behavior of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, NiFe-TDC, CQD, RuO2/NF and NF were evaluated by using a three-electrode system in a 1 M potassium hydroxide solution. To avoid the influence of oxidation peaks on the evaluation of the materials' electrocatalytic performance, a reverse scan (from high voltage to low voltage) was used for linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves in the voltage range of 1.0–0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibited a low overpotential of 222 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, demonstrating high OER performance, which significantly outperforms commercial RuO2/NF (296 mV), CQD (334 mV) and NiFe-TDC (231 mV). The results show that the combined action of CQD and NiFe-TDC markedly improves the electrocatalytic activity of OER. In addition, Fig. S3a-d show that the overpotential of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 at 10 mA cm−2 is also lower than those of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.01 (240 mV), CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.05 (240 mV), D-CQD@NiFe-TDC (237 mV) and E-CQD@NiFe-TDC (315 mV). In comparison with the recently reported results (Table S1 and Fig. 4h), CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 demonstrate the lowest overpotentials at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, to elucidate the OER kinetics of electrocatalysts, the Tafel slope derived from the LSV curve was obtained. Fig. 4c reveals that the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits a Tafel slope of 49.22 mV dec−1, a value considerably lower than that of CQD (164.7 mV dec−1) and NiFe-TDC (85.68 mV dec−1), indicating that CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 has good catalytic kinetic performance. The charge transfer resistance was evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). As shown in Fig. 4d, the electrocatalytic charge transfer resistance (Rct) value of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 is measured at 0.41 Ω, significantly less than that of NiFe-TDC (1.15 Ω) and CQD (111.60 Ω). This indicates that the nanoflowers structure of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 is beneficial to improve the interaction surface area of the electrode–electrolyte interface and accelerate the charge transfer rate. Furthermore, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the catalyst can be determined through double-layer capacitance (Cdl ). The Cdl value is evaluated from cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves. Fig. S4 is the CV curve of the catalyst at different scanning rates in the non-faradaic potential range of 0.09–0.19 V (vs RHE). The C dl value calculated by CV shows that the Cdl of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 is 2.03 mF cm−2, which is higher than that of NiFe-TDC (1.46 mF cm−2), but lower than that of CQD (46.5 mF cm−2). To further explore the relationship between the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the material and the catalytic performance, we further calculated the LSV curve under the electrochemical effective area (ECSA) (Fig. S5). CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits the highest catalytic performance, further indicating that it has excellent intrinsic electrochemical activity. The characteristic catalytic activities of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, NiFe-TDC and CQD were identified by determining the turnover frequencies (TOF) values of the redox surface area. Fig. 4f illustrates that CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 exhibits the maximum TOF value of 2.27 s−1, which is considerably higher than NiFe-TDC (0.19 s−1) and CQD (0.14 s−1). It suggests that CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 displays superior catalytic activity. In addition to excellent electrochemical activity, the stability is also a crucial indicator for evaluating catalyst performance. In order to evaluate the stability of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, a chronoamperometry test was conducted over a period of 100 h. As shown in Fig. 4g and S6, the overpotential of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 remained basically unchanged after 100 h at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, showing significant stability.

In order to deeply explore the mechanism underlying the exceptional OER performance of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, we analyze the evolution of the microstructure and composition of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 after the OER stability examination. The SEM images (Fig. 5 a-b) demonstrate that the nanosheets structure of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 is cross-linked with each other during an extended period of the OER stability test. This may be ascribed to the decomposition of NiFe-TDC during a prolonged OER stability experiment, resulting in the reconstruction of the surface into metal (oxide) hydroxide. However, no discernible NiOOH and FeOOH were observed in the XRD pattern (Fig. 5c), which may be attributed to the weak crystallinity and low contents of NiOOH and FeOOH. The XPS spectrum of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 indicates that the peak at 160.62 eV in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 5d) is attributed to M−S. [45] The peak corresponding to C-S-C bond disappears, which is attributed to the decomposition of NiFe-TDC and the formation of a metal (oxide) hydroxide. The observed peak at 167.54 eV is associated with the oxidized S species (denoted as SOx n−), suggesting that sulfate or sulfite is formed owing to partial oxidation of S. [46] Furthermore, the Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 5e) indicates that Ni0 disappears after the OER stability, accompanied by the formation of Ni2+. For the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 5f), the peak of Fe 2p3/2 shifts to a high binding energy, indicating the formation of high valence Fe ions. The results indicate that the surface of the pre-catalyst CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 undergoes the surface reconstruction to a highly active metal (oxide) hydroxide after the OER stability test. It can be reasonably concluded that the presence of metal (oxide) hydroxide serves as the real active sites to enhance the OER performance.

In comparison to the Earth's freshwater resources, seawater is a virtually inexhaustible source. Seawater and oceanic water account for approximately 96.5 % of the total water on the Earth. Seawater electrolysis represents a potential avenue for the large-scale production of hydrogen. [47] Additionally, in seawater systems under alkaline conditions, the competitive chlorine evolution reaction (ClER) occurs at a potential 480 mV higher than OER (1.23 V vs. RHE), leading to the production of hypochlorite and corrosion of the anode material, resulting in the poor stability. [48–50] In light of the outstanding OER performance exhibited by CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 catalyst in alkaline freshwater, the further investigation was conducted to assess the OER polarization curves of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 in 1.0 M KOH containing different amounts of NaCl. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a and 6c, the overpotentials of the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 is 266 and 267 mV at 100 mA cm−2. These values indicate that CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 catalyst still can continue the remarkable OER activity in simulated seawater. The OER catalytic performance of the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 catalyst in 1 M KOH seawater was evaluated at identical test conditions. The electrolyte was Bohai Sea water. Fig. 6b-c presents the LSV curve of the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 catalyst, which exhibited an overpotential of only 274 mV to reach a current density of 100 mA cm−2. Although the complex ion environment in seawater affects the OER performance, its overpotential is considerably lower than the theoretical prediction of the maximum overpotential of 480 mV needed to induce hypochlorite production. [51] In order to evaluate the stability of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 in seawater, a chronoamperometric analysis was conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 6d and e, the overpotential of the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 electrode was elevated from 274 to 306 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 for 100 h. Due to the formation of sulfate ions on the surface of the CQD@NiFe-MOF electrode during the long-term stable cycle, the formed sulfate ions can effectively repel Cl− ions and thus impeding their corrosion. Although the overpotential exhibited a slight increase, CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 demonstrated satisfactory OER durability in seawater. The SEM image (Fig. S7) presents that CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 nanoflowers become crosslinked nanosheets after a 100 h of OER durability test in alkaline seawater, indicating the high stability.

Given the high OER performance of CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03, the water electrolysis cell was constructed by using CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 and Pt/C as the positive electrode and the negative electrode, respectively, and an overall water splitting test was conducted in alkaline solution. Fig. 7 a illustrates that the CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03//Pt/C device requires a voltage of only 1.52 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is much lower than that of the RuO2//Pt/C electrode device (1.83 V). The overall water splitting stability of water electrolysis cell was evaluated through the chronoamperometry in a 1 M KOH solution (Fig. 7b and c). The results demonstrated that the voltage exhibited a slight elevate from 1.93 to 1.97 V after 100 h of the stability test at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, demonstrating the satisfactory durability.

In summary, CQD@NiFe-TDC composite catalytic materials were successfully synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method by using ferric citrate and H2TDC. Here, H2TDC serves as the corrosion agent and ligand to coordinate with metal ions, and ferric citrate is used as corrosion agent and carbon precursor to form CQD, which leads to in situ synthesizing CQD@NiFe-TDC electrocatalytic composites on a conductive NF substrate. Compared with the traditional synthesis method of CQD@MOF composites, [30,52] this method is simple and easy to large-scale production. The as-prepared CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 electrode exhibits the excellent OER capability under alkaline electrolyte and natural seawater with the overpotentials of 222 and 237 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively, which outperforms the traditional noble metal catalysts. [53–55] Furthermore, the assembled water electrolysis cell with CQD@NiFe-TDC-0.03 as the anode and Pt/C as the cathode only requires a voltage of 1.93 V to reach a current density of 100 mA cm−2. This work provides a simple and cost-effective method for the preparation of CQD@MOF composites to achieve efficient and stable water oxidation materials.

Yuzhen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Huajun Zhou: Visualization, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Peihua Zhao: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Kai Yuan: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Rui Zhou: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Hui Gao: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Yongping Qu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Yanzhong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
